text: The structural features of carbohelicenes have made them subjects of considerable research. Nowadays they are increasingly being used as components in the preparation of various materials with useful functions. In particular, their strong CPD signals attracted much interest for the design of novel light materials. While various methods, both enantioselective and not, have been reported for the preparation of enantiomeric fused carbo- and heterohelicenes, to the best of our knowledge, no catalyst-assisted method is reported for the preparation of the lower non-fused carbo[*n*]helicenes of interest for numerous applications and structural studies. Here we show an efficient general synthesis of these compounds by means of palladium-catalysed enantioselective C–H activation reaction employing a newly synthesised bifunctional bifluorophosphate–phosphine–carboxylate-ligand and theoretical insights reveal that the enantioselection is the result of the cooperation between C–H activation and the following reductive elimination. These non-covalent effects are sustained by severe steric repulsions operating between the catalyst–ligand complex and the molecular transition state of the less stable reductive elimination in each of the two successive cycles. The photophysical and chiroptical properties of the produced enantiomeric mixtures (scalemic) [*n*]helicenes have been carefully and systematically investigated.
